title: On the effect of randomness on planted 3-coloring models
speaker: Uri Feige
abstract: The random planted 3-coloring model generates a 3-colorable graph $G$ by first generating a random host graph $H$ of average degree $d$, and then planting in it a random 3-coloring (by giving each vertex a random color and dropping the monochromatic edges). For a sufficiently large constant $c$, Alon and Kahale [SICOMP 1997] presented a spectral algorithm that finds (with high probability) the planted 3-coloring of such graphs whenever $d > c\log n$.

proof_stages.label: Hosted planted three-coloring framework
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the model by choosing a host graph H, planting a vertex coloring P, deleting monochromatic edges, and asking an algorithm that sees only the resulting graph to output any legal three-coloring, with random and adversarial choices of H and P as special cases.

proof_stages.label: Baseline Alon–Kahale algorithm in the random-random model
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the known random-host/random-planting results, including high-density intuition, approximate and safe partial colorings, good and bad vertices, and the spectral-clustering/local-improvement/uncoloring/dynamic-programming pipeline.

proof_stages.label: Randomness-dependence questions and spectral-expander criteria
proof_stages.summary: The central questions are posed by isolating which parts of Alon–Kahale use host randomness versus planting randomness, leading to spectral conditions on the host graph and the case of an arbitrary expander with a random planted coloring.

proof_stages.label: Expander-host algorithm with random planting
proof_stages.summary: For an arbitrary spectral expander host and random planted coloring, essentially the same algorithm still works after replacing random-graph arguments by expansion analysis, safe recoloring, and a connected-subgraph counting lemma to complete the partial coloring in polynomial time.

proof_stages.label: Effect of adversarial planting on partial recovery
proof_stages.summary: When the planted coloring is adversarial but balanced, the good/bad-vertex estimates deteriorate, so the same spectral and local steps still yield approximate and safe partial colorings but leave a larger uncolored set whose completion becomes the key obstacle.

proof_stages.label: NP-hardness via a hidden sparse hard subgraph
proof_stages.summary: The hardness theorem shows that even a random G(n,d) host can be made NP-hard to three-color by an adversarial balanced planting for d=n^δ with 0.4<δ<0.5, using a reduction that hides a fixed sparse NP-hard graph q inside the random host and proves the adversarially generated distribution indistinguishable from a simulated disjoint union.

proof_stages.label: Phase diagram and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk synthesizes which combinations of random/adversarial host and planting admit spectral recovery, safe partial recovery, or legal completion, and highlights open regimes for low-degree random hosts with adversarial planting and arbitrary hosts with random planting.

examples.name: High-density common-neighbor clustering
examples.note: For very large average degree, common-neighbor counts separate same-color from different-color vertex pairs, illustrating why random planted colorings become algorithmically easy in dense regimes.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker reasons through the high-density case by comparing common-neighbor counts for same-color versus different-color vertex pairs and deriving the clustering idea and threshold.

passes.reason: The example is the very high-degree planted-coloring regime where common-neighbor counts recover the coloring, an extreme dense case rather than a refutation.
